_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 5-2 Adult Bradycardia (Revised 05/25/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Adult Cardiac Protocol Section 5-2: Bradycardia — MDHHS Approved 5/25/23, Reviewed 2023
purpose: This protocol is for PARAMEDIC USE ONLY. Intended for patients with serious symptomatic bradycardia defined as heart rate < 60 bpm with hypotension or shock. Titrate treatments to heart rate above 60 bpm. If patient remains hypotensive after treatments, refer to Shock-Treatment Protocol.

sections.title: Bradycardia Treatment
points: Administer atropine 1 mg IV/IO rapid push — repeat every 3–5 minutes to a total dose of 3 mg IV/IO until heart rate > 60/min

Transcutaneous pacing (TCP) when available may be initiated prior to IV access and/or before atropine begins to take effect — pacing is the TREATMENT OF CHOICE for high-degree AV block (second-degree Type II, or third-degree); apply pacer pads; follow Electrical Therapy-Procedure Protocol
Transcutaneous pacemaker electrode pads may be applied without initiating pacing so pacemaker is ready if patient condition deteriorates
For symptomatic high-degree (second-degree Type II or third-degree) AV block: begin pacing WITHOUT DELAY
Per MCA selection: provide sedation per Patient Procedural Sedation-Procedure Protocol

For persistent symptomatic bradycardia: administer epinephrine by push dose (dilute boluses) — preparation: add 1 mL of epinephrine 1 mg/10 mL in 9 mL NS to make 10 mcg/mL; administer 10–20 mcg (1–2 mL) IV/IO; repeat every 3–5 minutes; titrate SBP > 90 mmHg
NOTE: Heart transplant patients may NOT respond to atropine

mca_selections_required.description: MCA must select whether sedation may be administered per Patient Procedural Sedation-Procedure Protocol for transcutaneous pacing
options: Authorized per Patient Procedural Sedation-Procedure Protocol

key_drugs.name: Atropine 1 mg IV/IO (Bradycardia)
key_drugs.dose: 1 mg IV/IO rapid push — repeat every 3–5 minutes to maximum total dose of 3 mg; titrate HR > 60 bpm
key_drugs.indication: Symptomatic bradycardia (HR < 60 with hypotension or shock)
key_drugs.contraindications: High-degree AV block (2nd-degree Type II, 3rd-degree) — pacing preferred over atropine
key_drugs.side_effects: Tachycardia, dry mouth, urinary retention, paradoxical bradycardia with very low doses
key_drugs.notes: Heart transplant patients may NOT respond to atropine. Begin pacing without delay for high-degree AV block.

key_drugs.name: Push-Dose Epinephrine IV/IO (Persistent Bradycardia)
key_drugs.dose: 10–20 mcg (1–2 mL of 10 mcg/mL solution) IV/IO — repeat every 3–5 minutes; titrate SBP > 90 mmHg
key_drugs.indication: Persistent symptomatic bradycardia not responding to atropine and/or pacing
key_drugs.contraindications: Use caution in patients with suspected cardiogenic shock
key_drugs.notes: Preparation: add 1 mL of epinephrine 1 mg/10 mL (1:10,000) to 9 mL NS to make 10 mcg/mL concentration.

deterioration_flags: High-degree AV block (2nd-degree Type II or 3rd-degree) — begin TCP immediately WITHOUT DELAY; atropine less effective
Heart transplant with bradycardia — atropine will likely NOT work; proceed directly to pacing
Persistent hypotension after atropine and pacing — push-dose epinephrine; refer to Shock-Treatment Protocol
Suspected hyperkalemia (dialysis patient, EKG changes) — consider calcium chloride and sodium bicarbonate per Cardiac Arrest protocol guidance
scope_notes: This protocol is PARAMEDIC USE ONLY
Transcutaneous pacing: Paramedic — begin without delay for high-degree AV block
Sedation for TCP: per MCA selection per Patient Procedural Sedation-Procedure Protocol
